query: FDA approvals of CAR-T cell therapies for B-cell malignancies

retrieved_docs.title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clusters of differentiation-19 (CD-19), a B cell surface marker, -directed CAR-T cells show encouraging outcomes in a variety of malignancies, including pediatric and B-cell acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), and chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The first approved CAR-T therapy targeting CD-19, tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel, ELIANA), was initially approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for patients with ALL in 2017, and later diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), high-grade B-cell lymphoma, and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma (JULIET). [6] [7] [8] Around the same time, axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel, ZUMA-1) gained approval for relapsed/refractory (R/R) DLBCL and later for R/R follicular lymphoma. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The first CAR-T cell treatment approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration(FDA) in August 2017 was tisagenlecleucel. It is indicated in patients up to 25 years of age with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia that is refractory or in the second or later relapse. It also has indications in adult patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy, including diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) not otherwise specified, high-grade B-cell lymphoma and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma. The second US FDA-approved CAR-T cell treatment, in October 2017, is axicabtagene ciloleucel, which is indicated for the treatment of adult patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy, including DLBCL not otherwise specified, primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma, high-grade B-cell lymphoma, and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma. Neither treatment indicates patients with primary central nervous system(CNS) lymphoma. According to the US National Institute of Health clinical trial registration, multiple clinical trials are underway with CAR-T cell treatment for different malignancies, including multiple myeloma, CNS tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, lung cancer, etc. [3] [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since then, more agents such as lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel) have made their way onto the market, demonstrating better tolerance. [11] Later, brexucabtagene autoleucel was approved for R/R mantle cell lymphoma (MCL). [12] More recently, anti-B-cell Maturation Antigen (BCMA) CAR-T, idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel, KarMMa), and ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel, CARTITUDE-1) have been approved for R/R multiple myeloma. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: CAR T-cell therapy has primarily been investigated and approved for certain hematological malignancies, particularly B-cell malignancies. These include:

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: While CAR T-cell therapy has shown significant promise in treating certain types of cancer, particularly B-cell malignancies, ongoing research is needed to expand its applicability to other cancer types and improve its efficacy and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL): CAR T-cell therapy has demonstrated remarkable efficacy in treating relapsed or refractory B-ALL, particularly in pediatric and young adult patients. Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma (DLBCL): CAR T-cell therapy has been approved for certain subtypes of DLBCL, including relapsed or refractory DLBCL after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy. Follicular Lymphoma (FL): CAR T-cell therapy has demonstrated promising results in treating relapsed or refractory FL, especially in patients who have failed multiple lines of therapy. Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL): CAR T-cell therapy has shown efficacy in treating relapsed or refractory MCL, particularly in patients who have failed prior therapies. Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL): While still investigational, CAR T-cell therapy is being studied in CLL, particularly in patients with high-risk disease or those who have relapsed after multiple lines of therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Mantle Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib, venetoclax +/- rituximab, bortezomib +/- rituximab, lenalidomide plus rituximab, ibrutinib and lenalidomide + rituximab and chimeric antigen receptor T-cells (CAR-T) received FDA approval and several other agents including BiTE antibodies, antibody-drug conjugates and are under clinical trials. BCR- B cell receptor, BTK - Bruton's tyrosine kinase

retrieved_docs.title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals caring for patients receiving commercial CAR-T products must complete product-specific Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) training. [45] [46] [47] Nurses caring for these patients are educated to recognize CAR-T AEs, especially CRS and ICANS. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Chimeric antigen receptors usually contain an extracellular domain that binds to a specific antigen on tumor cells, a transmembrane domain, and intracellular domains that signal T-cell activation to attack tumor cells. Tisagenlecleucel is a cluster of differentiation (CD) 19-directed genetically modified autologous T-cell immunotherapy that involves reprogramming a patient’s T-cells with a transgene encoding a CAR to identify and eliminate CD19-expressing cells, both malignant and normal. The CAR comprises a murine single-chain antibody fragment; this recognizes CD19 and fuses to intracellular signaling domains from 4-1BB (CD137) and CD3 zeta. The CD3 zeta component is critical for initiating T-cell activation and anti-tumor activity, while 4-1BB enhances the expansion and persistence of tisagenlecleucel. Upon binding to CD19-expressing cells, the CAR transmits a signal to promote T-cell expansion, activation, target cell elimination, and persistence of the tisagenlecleucel cells. Axicabtagene ciloleucel is another CD19-directed genetically modified autologous T-cell immunotherapy that binds to CD19-expressing cancer cells and normal B cells. Following anti-CD19 CAR T-cell engagement with CD19-expressing target cells, the CD28 and CD3-zeta co-stimulatory domains activate downstream signaling cascades that lead to T-cell activation, proliferation, acquisition of effector functions and secretion of inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. This sequence of events leads to the killing of CD19-expressing cells. [3] [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for CART therapy. Determine the complications of CART therapy. Assess the contraindications of CART therapy. Communicate some interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination in patients who receive CART therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Chimeric antigen receptor-T (CAR-T) cell therapy is a type of genetically modified immunotherapy that is directed at cancer cells. The method involves using an individual's own T-cells (or donor cells in allogeneic CART), transduced with gene encodes ex vivo, then introduced to the patient. [1] CAR-T has changed the treatment paradigm for hematological malignancies, such as B-cell lymphomas and multiple myeloma, and continues to be a promising approach for many other malignancies, including solid tumors. [2] [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance -- Current FDA Approved Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Histone deacetylation leads to the repression of a specific gene. Drugs that inhibit deacetylation, known as histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi’s), lead to decreased histone deacetylation and ultimately increased gene expression. There are four HDACi’s currently approved by the FDA. Vorinostat obtained approval in 2006 for the treatment of cutaneous t-cell lymphoma. [9] Romidepsin is a natural HDACi isolated from bacterial fermentation of Chromobacterium violaceum and was approved in 2009 for the treatment of cutaneous t-cell lymphoma.  Belinostat received approval in 2014, and panobinostat approval came in 2015, both under the FDA’s accelerated approval program, for the treatment of relapsed peripheral t-cell lymphoma and relapsed multiple myeloma, respectively. [10] Many other HDACi’s are currently under investigation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Cytokine release syndrome (Severe or life-threatening, CAR T-Cell therapy) Patients ≥2 years of age with chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell-induced severe or life-threatening cytokine release syndrome (CRS)

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these hematological malignancies, CAR T-cell therapy is also being investigated for certain types of solid tumors, although progress in this area has been more limited. Solid tumors being targeted with CAR T-cell therapy include: Multiple Myeloma: CAR T-cell therapy targeting BCMA (B-cell maturation antigen) has shown promise in treating relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma. Glioblastoma: CAR T-cell therapy targeting EGFRvIII (epidermal growth factor receptor variant III) has been investigated in glioblastoma, although results have been mixed. Sarcomas: CAR T-cell therapy is being explored in certain types of sarcomas, including osteosarcoma and synovial sarcoma, although research is still in the early stages.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical Applications: CAR T-cell therapy has shown remarkable success in treating certain hematological malignancies, such as B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) and certain types of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). It is also being investigated for solid tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 464
retrieved_docs.content: dual classification as an antineoplastic drug and a non‑steroidal abortifacient, highlighting its multiple pharmacologic actions. Collectively, these relationships delineate the therapeutic options, contraindications, and pharmacologic classifications pertinent to the treatment and management of leukemia and illustrate the multifaceted nature of antitumor substances, which encompass traditional cytotoxic drugs, corticosteroid derivatives, nucleic‑acid‑based compounds, vaccines, and emerging biologic or immunologic therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 765
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of targeted therapies that block the BTK enzyme, a key component of B‑cell receptor signaling. Ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are both members of this BTK inhibitor class, sharing the same mechanism of action. Ibrutinib may treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia) by inhibiting malignant B‑cell proliferation. Recognizing that both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib belong to the BTK inhibitor family informs clinicians when selecting appropriate targeted agents for patients with B‑cell malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1304
retrieved_docs.content: Perfenazin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب خلايا البلازما (plasma cell disorder), meaning that the drug should not be prescribed to individuals with this condition to avoid potential adverse effects. This contraindication underscores the importance of reviewing a patient’s history for plasma cell disorders before initiating perfenazin therapy, ensuring safer medication selection and better patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.
